issues.title: Client-controlled order price accepted without server-side validation
issues.description: The POST /api/checkout/orders route accepts `total_cop` from the request body and uses it directly to record the order total (line 90: `const totalCop = total_cop`). The only check is that it is a positive finite number (lines 81-83). No server-side re-calculation of the correct price from the product catalog is performed. The price originates from the client-side cart cookie (`price_cop` stored by the browser), which an attacker can freely modify before submitting a checkout.
issues.impact: An authenticated user can craft an HTTP request with an arbitrarily low `total_cop` (e.g., 1 COP) and create a valid order at that manipulated price. The order is persisted to the database with the tampered total, and the seller's payment instructions are returned via the payment-methods endpoint (which validates stock but not price). This allows buyers to commit to orders at fraudulent prices.
issues.fix: Server-side: After fetching the payment method (which resolves `seller_id`), query `products` for the submitted item IDs, compute the expected `total_cop` from DB prices × quantities, and reject the request if the submitted `total_cop` does not match. If this route does not receive cart items (only `payment_method_id`), add a separate validation step or pass cart items and recompute on the server.

issues.title: payment_method_id used in PostgREST URL without UUID validation
issues.description: In `fetchPaymentMethod`, the `payment_method_id` string from the request body is embedded into a PostgREST REST URL using `encodeURIComponent` (line 18: `seller_payment_methods?id=eq.${encodeURIComponent(paymentMethodId)}`). While `encodeURIComponent` prevents most URL-level injection, there is no UUID format validation applied to `payment_method_id` before this call. The payment-methods route (route.ts:190-193) validates `sellerId` and items with `UUID_REGEX`, but the orders route skips this step for `payment_method_id`. A malformed (non-UUID) value is passed to the database query rather than being rejected early at the API boundary.
issues.impact: Minor: PostgREST will return an empty result for a non-UUID `id` due to type mismatch (Postgres UUID column), so there is no SQL injection risk in practice. However, the inconsistency allows unusual values into the query layer, produces harder-to-diagnose errors, and could interact unexpectedly with future query changes.
issues.fix: Add UUID validation at the start of the handler, the same way it is done in the payment-methods route: validate `payment_method_id` with `UUID_REGEX` before calling `fetchPaymentMethod`. Example: `if (!UUID_REGEX.test(payment_method_id)) return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Invalid payload' }, { status: 400 });`

issues.title: Unvalidated user-supplied URL rendered as clickable link and image source
issues.description: The `BuyerInfoValue` component renders any `buyer_info` value that starts with `https://` as a clickable `` link (line 52-62). If the value also matches an image extension pattern it is rendered as an `` tag with the attacker-controlled URL as `src` (lines 36-50). The `buyer_info` values come from buyer-submitted form fields stored in the database without URL allowlist validation on the server side. While React's JSX rendering prevents XSS in attribute values, a malicious seller or a crafted form submission can: (1) cause external image loads that leak the viewer's IP to a tracking server via the `src`, (2) load harmful content (e.g. exploit images in older browsers), and (3) craft phishing links that appear to originate from the platform UI.
issues.impact: IP leakage / tracking pixel via automatic image loads; phishing links displayed in the seller's order management view; potential SSRF if the app's server ever pre-fetches these URLs. Primarily affects sellers viewing received orders.
issues.fix: Server-side: validate `buyer_info` URL values against an allowlist of trusted upload domains (e.g. your Supabase storage bucket hostname) before saving to the database. Client-side as a defence-in-depth: replace the open `isUrl` check (`value.startsWith('https://')`) with a strict domain allowlist check, and add `crossOrigin="anonymous"` and `referrerPolicy="no-referrer"` to the `` tag. Consider adding `sandbox` attribute if rendering in an iframe context.

patterns.description: Consistent UUID validation via validateUuid() across admin routes and payment-methods route prevents path parameter injection.
Auth refresh endpoint sets access token as HTTP-only cookie, not in response body. CORS uses an explicit allowlist of known app origins.
OAuth callback (handleOAuthCallback) validates redirect destination with isSafeRedirect() and an origins allowlist, preventing open redirect.
No dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage found anywhere in the apps/ directory.
Service role key is read from process.env and never hardcoded; all admin REST clients read credentials from environment variables only.
Admin API routes gate all operations behind getAuthorizedAdmin() which checks both session validity and required permission keys.
